content: Fundamentals: Small Habits Bring Big Changes
title: Reading Atomic Habits

content: The surprising power of tiny habitsExample: British Cycling hired a new performance director who led the team onto a new path. Unlike previous directors, he meticulously carried out a strategy he called the aggregation of marginal gains, and it brought the cycling team enormous success. Conclusion: Small habits can bring big changes. Question: Why? Conclusion: Habits are the compound interest of self-improvement. Over time, the effects of small habits intensify. The long-term impact of small habits Conclusion: Slow transformation also makes it easy for bad habits to linger. Conclusion: Your outcomes are lagging measures of your habits. Your net worth is a lagging measure of your financial habits. Your weight is a lagging measure of your eating habits. Your knowledge is a lagging measure of your learning habits. The clutter in your living environment is a lagging measure of your housekeeping habits. What you get is the result of what you repeatedly do day after day, year after year. Example: There is a block of ice on your desk. The room is cold, currently 25 degrees Fahrenheit. The room slowly warms up: 26, 27, 28, 29 … until 32 degrees, when the ice begins to melt. The temperature has only risen by one more degree, which seems no different from the previous increases, but it causes a huge change. Conclusion: Breakthrough moments are usually the result of a series of previous actions. Those actions accumulate the potential needed to trigger major change. Example: The San Antonio Spurs are one of the most successful teams in NBA history. In their locker room hangs a quote from social reformer Jacob Riis: when he feels powerless, he goes to watch a stonecutter hammering at a rock. Perhaps the stonecutter strikes it 100 times without a crack appearing, but on the 101st blow the stone splits in two. He knows it was not the final blow that did it, but all the blows that came before. Conclusion: After we put in effort, results often appear with a delay. It may take months or years before we recognize the true value of our earlier work. A crack after potential has accumulated Question: What determines whether we can persist long enough to pass through the plateau of latent potential until a breakthrough becomes part of our habits? And what causes some people to fall into bad habits while others enjoy the compound effects of good habits? Conclusion: We should forget goals and follow systems instead. Goals are about the results you want to achieve, while systems are about the processes that lead to those results. Question: If you focus all your attention on goals but neglect the systems behind them, several problems appear. Winners and losers have the same goals. Achieving a goal is only a temporary change. Goals restrict your happiness. Goals conflict with long-term progress. Conclusion: If you find it hard to change your habits, the problem is not you; it is your system. Bad habits repeat themselves not because you do not want to change, but because the system you use for change has problems.
title: Reading Atomic Habits

content: How your habits shape your identity, and vice versaConclusion: Changing habits is challenging for two reasons: (1) we try to change the wrong thing; (2) we try to change our habits in the wrong way. This chapter discusses the first point. Later chapters discuss the second. Conclusion: We can divide behavior change into three levels, like the layers of an onion. Outcomes, processes, and identity Conclusion: Outcomes are about what you get. Processes are about what you do. Identity is about what you believe. Conclusion: Outcome-based habits focus on what you want to achieve; identity-based habits focus on who you want to become. Different directions of habit change Example: Imagine two people refusing a cigarette. When offered one, the first person says, “No thanks, I am trying to quit.” This sounds reasonable, but it implies that the person still believes they are a smoker who is trying to become something else. They assume their behavior will change while they still hold that belief. The second person says, “No thanks, I do not smoke.” This response is slightly different, but it signals a shift in identity. Smoking is part of their past, not their present. They no longer see themselves as a smoker. Conclusion: The ultimate form of intrinsic motivation is when a habit becomes part of your identity. Saying “I want to be this kind of person” is one thing; saying “I am this kind of person” is another. Example: The goal is not to read a book, but to become a reader. The goal is not to run a marathon, but to become a runner. The goal is not to learn an instrument, but to become a musician. Conclusion: Every action you take is a vote for the type of person you want to become. One or two votes will not transform your beliefs, but as the votes accumulate, the evidence for your new identity changes. This is one reason meaningful change does not require dramatic change. Conclusion: I am the kind of strong person with a healthy body. I am a great programmer. I am a professional who can speak English fluently.
title: Reading Atomic Habits

content: The four-step model for building good habitsQuestion: What is a habit? Why does the brain bother building them? Conclusion: A habit is a behavior that has been repeated enough times to become automatic. The process of habit formation begins with repeated attempts. Conclusion: Habits are mental shortcuts learned from experience. In a sense, a habit is just the memory of the steps you previously took to solve a problem. Habit formation is useful because conscious attention is the bottleneck of the brain. We cannot focus on everything at once, so the brain always tries to direct attention toward the most important problem at the moment. Whenever possible, the mind consciously hands certain tasks to the unconscious so they can be completed automatically. This is exactly what happens when habits form. Habits reduce cognitive load and free mental capacity, allowing you to focus on other tasks. Conclusion: The habit-forming process can be divided into four simple steps: cue, craving, response, and reward. Cue, craving, response, reward Conclusion: We can turn these four steps into a practical framework for building good habits and eliminating bad ones. Whenever you want to change your behavior, ask yourself a few simple questions: How can I make it obvious? How can I make it attractive? How can I make it easy? How can I make it satisfying?
title: Reading Atomic Habits

content: The First Law: Make It Obvious
title: Reading Atomic Habits

content: Understand a habit before changing itConclusion: Over time, the cues that trigger our habits become so common that we are almost blind to them. Conclusion: As psychologist Carl Jung said, until you make the unconscious conscious, it will direct your life and you will call it fate. Example: In the Japanese railway system, drivers point at different objects and call out instructions while trains are running. It looks almost like a ritual. This process is called pointing and calling, a safety system designed to reduce human error. It may look silly, but it is extremely effective. Pointing and calling reduces errors by up to 85% and reduces accident rates by 30%. The New York City subway system adopted a modified version that kept only the pointing part; within two years, improper train stops were reduced by 57%. Example: The habit scorecard. Recording and observing habits Conclusion: The first step to breaking a bad habit is to stay aware of it. If you need extra help, you can try pointing and calling in your own life. Say out loud the action you are about to take and the result you expect. Example: If you want to stop eating junk food but notice that you have picked up a cookie, you can say out loud: “I am about to eat this cookie, but I do not need it. Eating it will make me gain weight and harm my health.”
title: Reading Atomic Habits

content: The most common cues are time and locationExample: In a 2001 exercise study, British researchers worked with 248 people for two weeks to help them build better exercise habits. The participants were divided into three groups. The first was the control group; they only tracked how often they exercised. The second was the motivation group; they tracked their exercise and read material about the benefits of exercise. Researchers also explained how exercise reduces the risk of coronary heart disease and improves heart health. The third group received the same motivational material as the second, so the two groups had the same level of motivation. However, the researchers also asked the third group to make a specific plan for when and where they would exercise during the coming week. Each person completed the sentence: “During the next week, I will exercise vigorously for at least 20 minutes on [day] at [time] in [place].” In the first and second groups, 35% to 38% of people exercised at least once per week. Interestingly, the motivational material seemed to have no real effect. But in the third group, 91% exercised at least once per week, more than double the normal rate. The completed sentence is called an implementation intention: a plan made in advance about when and where to act. Conclusion: Whether it is writing down the exact date and time for a flu shot or recording the appointment time for a colonoscopy, implementation intentions are an effective way to make sure we do not forget our original intention. Conclusion: You need to give your habit a time and place to exist in the world. The point is to make time and location so obvious that, after enough repetitions, you feel pulled to do the right thing at the right time, even if you cannot explain why. Example: The Diderot Effect. Many human behaviors follow this loop. You often decide what to do next based on what you just finished doing. Going to the bathroom leads to washing and drying your hands, which reminds you to put the dirty towel in the laundry basket, which then adds laundry detergent to your shopping list, and so on. No behavior happens in isolation. Each action becomes a cue for the next one. Conclusion: The best way to build a new habit is to identify an existing habit and stack the new behavior on top of it. This is called habit stacking. Performing a new habit after a current habit Conclusion: The key is to connect the behavior you want with something you already do every day. Once you master this basic structure, you can create larger habit stacks by linking small habits together. Example: Your morning routine might look like this: 1. After pouring my morning coffee, I will meditate for 60 seconds. 2. After meditating for 60 seconds, I will write my to-do list for the day. 3. After writing my to-do list, I will immediately start the first task. Or your evening habit stack might look like this: 1. After dinner, I will put the dishes directly into the dishwasher. 2. After clearing the dishes, I will immediately wipe down the kitchen counter. 3. After wiping the counter, I will prepare the coffee cup I need the next morning. Conclusion: The implementation intention formula is: I will [behavior] at [time] in [location]. Conclusion: The habit stacking formula is: After [current habit], I will [new habit].
title: Reading Atomic Habits

content: Environment often mattersConclusion: People choose records not because of what they are, but because they are there. Conclusion: Environment is the invisible hand that shapes human behavior. Although we each have unique personalities, certain behaviors repeatedly appear under specific environmental conditions. Example: If you want to remember to take medicine every night, put the pill bottle directly on the bathroom counter near the faucet. If you want to practice guitar more often, place your guitar stand in the center of the living room. If you want to remember to send more thank-you notes, put a stack of note cards on your desk. If you want to drink more water, fill several water bottles every morning and place them in visible spots around the house.
title: Reading Atomic Habits

content: The Second Law: Make It Attractive
title: Reading Atomic Habits

content: Habits do not resistExample: After hundreds of thousands of years of hunting and foraging in the wild, the human brain evolved to place great value on salt, sugar, and fat. These foods are usually high in calories, and they were very rare when our ancestors wandered the savanna. When you live without knowing when the next meal will come, eating as much as possible whenever you get the chance is an excellent survival strategy. Example: Interestingly, the reward system activated in the brain when you receive a reward is the same system activated when you anticipate a reward. This is one reason anticipation of an experience is often more pleasurable than the experience itself. As a child, looking forward to Christmas morning may feel better than actually opening the presents; as an adult, imagining an upcoming vacation may feel more exciting than the vacation itself. Scientists call this the difference between wanting and liking. Conclusion: Temptation bundling is one way to apply Premack’s Principle, named after professor David Premack. The principle states that high-frequency behaviors reinforce low-frequency behaviors. In other words, even if you genuinely do not want to handle overdue work emails, you may get used to doing it if it means you can simultaneously do something you really want to do. Example: The formula for habit stacking plus temptation bundling is: 1. After [current habit], I will [habit I need]. 2. After [habit I need], I will [habit I want]. You really want to read the news, but you need to express more gratitude: 1. After morning coffee, I will say one thing from yesterday that I am grateful for. 2. After saying the thing I am grateful for, I may read the news. You really want to watch sports, but you need to make sales calls: 1. After returning from lunch, I will call three potential clients. 2. After calling three potential clients, I will check ESPN for game updates. You really want to check Facebook, but you need more exercise: 1. After taking out my phone, I will do ten burpees. 2. After doing ten burpees, I can check Facebook updates.
title: Reading Atomic Habits

content: The role of friends and family in habitsExample: Laszlo’s plan to train his children into chess prodigies. Conclusion: We pay special attention to imitating the habits of three groups: People close to us The majority The powerful
title: Reading Atomic Habits

content: How to find and remove the root cause of bad habitsExample: You think you are giving something up, but in fact you are not giving up anything, because cigarettes offer you no real benefit. You think smoking is something you need for social situations, but it is not. You can socialize perfectly well without smoking. You think smoking relieves stress, but it does not. Smoking does not calm your nerves; it damages your nervous system. Conclusion: Every behavior has a surface-level craving and a deeper motive. Your habits are modern methods for satisfying ancient desires, new forms of old impulses. Habits are associations. These associations determine whether we think a habit is worth repeating.
title: Reading Atomic Habits

content: The Third Law: Make It EasyExample: A professor divided his film photography students into two groups: one group pursued quantity, the other pursued quality. In the end, the quantity group won. Conclusion: What you should focus on is the number of repetitions. Conclusion: To build a habit, you need practice. The most effective way to stick with practice is to follow the third law of behavior change: make it easy. Example: Look at any behavior that takes up a large portion of your life, and you will find that it is easy and convenient. Habits such as checking your phone, checking email, and watching television take up a lot of time because they require almost no effort. Example: Imagine watering a garden with a hose that is bent in the middle, causing the water to flow poorly. If you want to increase the flow, you have two choices: open the valve all the way and increase water pressure, or simply straighten the hose and let more water flow naturally. Imagine watering a garden with a hose that is bent in the middle, causing the water to flow poorly. If you want to increase the flow, you have two choices: open the valve all the way and increase water pressure, or simply straighten the hose and let more water flow naturally. Example: Nuckols developed a strategy he called resetting the room, and through consistent practice he gradually built a cleaning habit. For example, after watching television, he would put the remote back on the TV stand, straighten the sofa pillows, and fold the blanket. After getting out of the car, he would throw away all the trash inside. Whenever he showered, he would scrub the toilet while the water heated up. As he put it, the best time to clean the toilet is right before you shower anyway. The point of resetting the room is not only to clean up after one task, but to make the next task easier.
title: Reading Atomic Habits

content: Stop procrastinatingExample: Every evening, there is a tiny moment, usually around 5:15 p.m., that sets the tone for my entire night. After my wife gets home from work, we either change into workout clothes and go to the gym, or we order Indian takeout and lie on the sofa watching The Office. Just like Twyla Tharp calling a cab, changing into workout clothes is our ritual. If I change clothes, I know the next step is going to the gym. Once I take the first step, everything else becomes easy: driving to the gym, choosing an activity, lifting weights. The key moment that starts a new habit From starting to continuing When you start building a new habit, it should take no more than two minutes. Many habits happen at decisive moments. Each choice is like a fork in the road, and your choice can eventually lead to a productive day or a wasted one. The more ritualized the beginning of a routine is, the more likely you are to enter the focused state needed to do great work.
title: Reading Atomic Habits

content: Make bad habits difficultExample: Victor Hugo made a strange plan to overcome procrastination. He gathered all his clothes and had an assistant lock them in a large chest. Except for a large shawl, he had nothing to wear. During the autumn and winter of 1830, because he had no suitable clothes for going out, he stayed in his study and wrote intensely. Notre-Dame de Paris was published on January 14, 1831, two weeks ahead of schedule. Conclusion: Sometimes success is not simply about making good habits easy. More importantly, it is about making bad habits hard to continue. This is the inversion of the third law of behavior change: make it difficult.
title: Reading Atomic Habits

content: The Fourth Law: Make It Satisfying
title: Reading Atomic Habits

content: The basic principle of behavior changeThe key to turning immediate satisfaction in your favor is to create a sense of achievement, even if it is only a small feeling. Example: One way to solve this problem is to reverse it. Make the habit you want to avoid visible. Open a savings account and label it for something you really want to buy in the future, such as a leather jacket. Every time you give up buying something, transfer the corresponding amount of money into that account. Did not buy coffee this morning? Deposit 5 dollars. Did not renew Netflix last month? Deposit 10 dollars. It is like creating a loyalty program for yourself. Seeing the immediate reward of saving money for a leather jacket feels much better than the feeling of giving up shopping. In this way, even if you buy nothing, you still feel satisfied. One of my readers and his wife did this. They wanted to reduce how often they ate out and started cooking together at home. They labeled their savings account “Trip to Europe.” Every time they skipped eating out, they transferred 50 dollars into the account. At the end of the year, they spent the money on a vacation. It is worth noting that you must choose short-term rewards that reinforce your identity, not ones that conflict with it. If you want to lose weight or read more books, buying a new jacket is fine, but it is not suitable if your goal is simply to save money. Likewise, taking a bath or wandering around to enjoy your leisure time can be a good reward if it aligns with your pursuit of more freedom and financial independence. If your reward for exercising is a bowl of ice cream, you are undermining yourself and eventually canceling out your efforts. Instead, you might reward yourself with a full-body massage, which is both a luxury and a way to take care of your body. Now the short-term reward matches your long-term vision of staying healthy.
title: Reading Atomic Habits

content: How to keep good habits every dayProgress is satisfying. With visual measures such as moving paper clips, hairpins, or marbles, you can clearly see your progress. As a result, they reinforce your behavior and add immediate satisfaction to any activity. Visual measures come in many forms: food journals, workout logs, punch-card loyalty programs, software download progress bars, and even page numbers in books. But perhaps the best way to measure progress is habit tracking. How do you recover quickly when your habits break down? Never miss twice. If I miss one day, I will get back on track as quickly as possible.
title: Reading Atomic Habits

content: How an accountability partner can change everythingFortunately, there is a shortcut that directly increases the immediate cost of any bad habit: create a habit contract. Governments can change our habits by creating social contracts, and laws and regulations are good examples. In society, we collectively agree to follow certain rules and enforce them together. Whenever new legislation is introduced, such as seat belt laws, smoking bans in restaurants, or mandatory recycling, it affects people’s behavior and becomes an example of social contracts shaping our habits. The group collectively agrees to follow a certain behavior, and if you do not follow it, you are punished. Just as governments use laws to hold citizens accountable, you can create a habit contract to hold yourself responsible. A habit contract is a verbal or written agreement in which you declare your commitment to a specific habit and agree to accept a corresponding punishment if you break your promise. Then you find one or two people as accountability partners and sign the agreement with them.
title: Reading Atomic Habits

content: Summary of Methods
title: Reading Atomic Habits

content: How to build good habits Category Method Specific practice First law: Make it obvious 1.1 Fill out a habit scorecard; write down your current habits and notice them 1.2 Use implementation intentions: “I will [behavior] at [time] in [location].” 1.3 Use habit stacking: “After [current habit], I will [new habit].” 1.4 Design your environment. Make the cues for good habits clear and obvious Second law: Make it attractive 2.1 Use temptation bundling. Reinforce the action you need with a behavior you enjoy 2.2 Join a culture where your desired behavior is normal 2.3 Create a motivation ritual. Do something you especially enjoy before a low-frequency action Third law: Make it easy 3.1 Reduce friction. Reduce the steps required to build a good habit 3.2 Prepare the environment. Create an environment that supports future behavior 3.3 Master the decisive moment. Optimize the small choices that create major impact 3.4 Use the two-minute rule. Shorten your habit until it takes two minutes or less 3.5 Automate your habits. Invest in technology and tools that lock in future behavior Fourth law: Make it satisfying 4.1 Use reinforcement. Reward yourself immediately after completing a habit routine 4.2 Make doing nothing enjoyable. When avoiding a bad habit, design a way to make the benefit visible 4.3 Use habit tracking. Record habit tendencies and do not break the chain 4.4 Never miss twice. If you forget once, recover as soon as possible
title: Reading Atomic Habits

content: The four laws for breaking bad habits, inverted Category Method Specific practice Inversion of the first law: Make it invisible 1.5 Reduce exposure. Remove the cues of bad habits from your environment Inversion of the second law: Make it unattractive 2.4 Reframe your mindset. List the benefits of quitting a bad habit Inversion of the third law: Make it difficult 3.6 Increase friction. Add steps before performing the bad habit 3.7 Use a commitment device. Lock in future choices that benefit you Inversion of the fourth law: Make it unsatisfying 4.5 Find an accountability partner. Ask someone to supervise your behavior 4.6 Create a habit contract. Make the consequences of a bad habit public and painful
title: Reading Atomic Habits

content: Advanced Tactics: How to Evolve From Merely Good to Truly Great
title: Reading Atomic Habits

content: Talent, Genetics and HabitsThe secret to maximizing your odds of success is picking the right arena in which to compete. Choose the right habits, and progress comes effortlessly. Choose the wrong ones, and life becomes an uphill struggle at every turn. Genetics are hard to alter. They grant you formidable advantages in favorable environments, yet impose distinct disadvantages when circumstances work against you. When your habits align with your natural talents, building and sustaining those habits comes naturally. Pick the habits that suit you best. Choose pursuits that let your strengths shine. If you cannot find one, create your own. Genetics do not eliminate the need for hard work. They only act as a filter, showing us what we ought to devote our efforts to.
title: Reading Atomic Habits

content: Burnout Is the Critical RiskThe Goldilocks Rule states that people maintain peak motivation when tackling tasks within their reach. The greatest threat to success is not failure, but burnout. As habits turn into routine, they grow less engaging and rewarding. Boredom creeps in. Anyone can grind away when motivated. Only the elite keep pushing forward when the work loses its thrill. Professionals stick rigidly to their set plans; amateurs drift along, acting on whims.
title: Reading Atomic Habits

content: The Hidden Downsides of Cultivating Good HabitsThe upside of habits is that we can act on autopilot. The downside is that we stop noticing minor mistakes. Habitual execution + Deliberate practice = Mastery Reflection and review form a process that keeps you attuned to your own performance at all times. The more deeply we cling to a fixed self-image, the harder it becomes to grow beyond it.
title: Reading Atomic Habits

content: Notes on my personal openSUSE and KDE installation and configuration process
title: My openSUSE Tumbleweed and KDE Installation Notes

content: Before InstallationThere is not much to say here. I like using Ventoy to create a bootable USB drive, then copying the openSUSE Tumbleweed image onto it.
title: My openSUSE Tumbleweed and KDE Installation Notes

content: KDE Plasma Desktop ConfigurationMy Plasma configuration is very simple. It mainly consists of the following steps: Move the panel to the top and reduce its size to 40. Go to Settings > Appearance > Icons and choose an icon pack I like. The default icon pack looks a little ugly to me, so I use Papirus. Go to Settings > Workspace Behavior > Virtual Desktops and adjust the virtual desktop layout. I set the number of rows to 1, and the default 2 virtual desktops are enough. Go to Settings > Window Management > Task Switcher and change the task switching effect from Breeze to Compact. It is the simplest switcher effect. I also remove the Alt + ` shortcut. Go to Settings > Input Devices > Keyboard > Advanced, enable Configure keyboard options, then enable Make Caps Lock an additional Ctrl. Of course we want to completely turn Caps Lock into Ctrl. After logging into the system, KDE Plasma users may see a KDE Wallet error the first time they connect to a wireless network because a key is missing. To avoid entering an authentication password repeatedly, open KDE Wallet, choose to create a new wallet, select the traditional Blowfish encrypted file format, leave the password empty, and confirm. This gives you an empty KDE Wallet with no authentication password, so connecting to the network no longer requires identity verification.
title: My openSUSE Tumbleweed and KDE Installation Notes

content: YaST Configuration and Repository ChangesFirst, install the Simplified Chinese language package. In YaST > Language > Secondary Language, select Simplified Chinese to install it. Next, open Software Repositories and remove the local repository that comes with the installation, something like openSUSE-20240216-0. Then switch the official repositories to the USTC openSUSE mirror. Because openSUSE uses MirrorBrain, the central server (download.opensuse.org) redirects download requests to nearby mirrors based on IP geolocation. However, repository metadata is still fetched from the central server when refreshing repositories, so changing mirrors usually speeds up repository refreshes more than actual package downloads. Then add the Packman repository directly in the terminal: bash ruby sudo zypper ar -cfp 90 https://mirrors.ustc.edu.cn/packman/suse/openSUSE_Tumbleweed/ USTC:PACKMAN After that, refresh and update Tumbleweed: bash bash sudo zypper ref && sudo zypper dup sudo reboot Then install some multimedia codecs: bash bash sudo zypper dist-upgrade --from USTC:PACKMAN --allow-vendor-change sudo zypper install --from USTC:PACKMAN ffmpeg gstreamer-plugins-{good,bad,ugly,libav} libavcodec-full vlc-codecs
title: My openSUSE Tumbleweed and KDE Installation Notes

content: Network Proxy ConfigurationI use v2raya for network proxying. First install the v2ray core and v2raya: bash bash sudo zypper in v2ray opi v2raya After starting the v2raya service, follow the quick start guide to use the proxy. Transparent proxying can provide proxy access for almost all programs. bash bash sudo systemctl enable --now v2raya.service For more proxy usage methods, see Configuring proxies.
title: My openSUSE Tumbleweed and KDE Installation Notes

content: Fonts and Chinese Input MethodInstalling fonts in KDE is easy: drag the fonts into Font Management, preferably into the Personal Fonts group. Then refresh the font cache with fc-cache -fv. The more traditional method is to place fonts in the following directories, listed roughly by priority: bash swift Font directories: /usr/X11R6/lib/X11/fonts /opt/kde3/share/fonts ~/.local/share/fonts ~/.local/share/flatpak/exports/share/fonts /var/lib/flatpak/exports/share/fonts /usr/local/share/fonts /usr/share/fonts ~/.fonts /usr/share/fonts/100dpi /usr/share/fonts/75dpi /usr/share/fonts/Type1 /usr/share/fonts/cyrillic /usr/share/fonts/encodings /usr/share/fonts/ghostscript /usr/share/fonts/misc /usr/share/fonts/texlive-lm /usr/share/fonts/truetype /usr/share/fonts/xscreensaver /usr/share/fonts/encodings/large I recommend putting fonts in ~/.fonts or ~/.local/share/fonts, then refreshing them with fc-cache -fv. My recommended font is Sarasa Term SC Nerd, because it handles table alignment well in Emacs and includes Nerd Fonts, which saves some trouble. For input methods, I recommend installing Fcitx5: bash bash sudo zypper in fcitx5 fcitx5-chinese-addons sudo zypper in fcitx5-configtool # install the configuration tool (optional) When installing Fcitx5, the old Fcitx will be removed and replaced by default. Just follow the system prompts. After installation, log in again. Then go to Settings > Regional Settings > Input Method and click Add Input Method to add Pinyin. If you are using KDE on X11, the required environment variables are already set automatically by the system. You can also click Configure addons to adjust the input method UI and related settings. Dictionaries can improve the input method experience. Here are two popular dictionaries: felixonmars/fcitx5-pinyin-zhwiki wuhgit/CustomPinyinDictionary Follow the README files in those repositories and place the downloaded or extracted .dict files into ~/.local/share/fcitx5/pinyin/dictionaries/. If the directory does not exist, create it manually, then restart fcitx5.
title: My openSUSE Tumbleweed and KDE Installation Notes

content: Installing SoftwareHere is a list of some software I install at the beginning. This is just a casual list; install what you need: bash less Install with zypper: git emacs opi keepassxc mpv telegram-desktop qbittorrent filezilla wireshark flameshot # installation guide (https://zh.opensuse.org/Flameshot) cheat nodejs syncthing Install with opi: megasync google-chrome-stable v2raya
title: My openSUSE Tumbleweed and KDE Installation Notes

content: Power Management for LaptopsThe default TLP configuration is fine. You can use TLPUI for easier configuration. bash bash sudo zypper in tlp sudo systemctl disable tlp && sudo systemctl stop tlp sudo systemctl enable tlp In TLPUI, I simply configure the charging thresholds, which can help extend laptop battery life a little: bash ini START_CHARGE_THRESH_BAT0=75 # start charging threshold STOP_CHARGE_THRESH_BAT0=85 # stop charging threshold Use the following command to check battery status: bash bash sudo tlp-stat -b
title: My openSUSE Tumbleweed and KDE Installation Notes

content: Terminal EmulatorUse whichever terminal emulator feels comfortable. I used to spend a lot of time tweaking shells, but now I am too lazy to configure them, so the default bash is fine. Installing Oh My Bash can make it look nicer. At the moment, my favorite shell may actually be Eshell inside Emacs. Also, if I use zsh, I personally dislike Oh My Zsh and would switch to a lighter configuration framework such as zim. For terminal emulators, I have been using kitty. It has good color support and includes kitten plugins. Whenever I set up a new machine, I can use kitten directly to configure themes and other settings. It is also very fast. My favorite terminal font is CaskaydiaMono Nerd Font. Also, the newer terminal emulator Warp was already available on Linux when I wrote this article. I have always liked using this very modern terminal on macOS. For terminal emulator configuration, changing the font is usually enough. One more thing: I really dislike transparent terminal backgrounds.
title: My openSUSE Tumbleweed and KDE Installation Notes

content: References openSUSE Wiki quick configuration guide openSUSE Wiki battery management v2raya user documentation
title: My openSUSE Tumbleweed and KDE Installation Notes

content: Notes on several ways to use Denote
title: How to Use Denote

content: AboutThese are casual notes on how to use Denote. Most of the content is translated or adapted directly from the official documentation.
title: How to Use Denote

content: ReferencesDenote documentation
title: How to Use Denote

content: Creating Standard NotesCommands that prompt the user for input when creating notes include, but are not limited to: denote denote-signature denote-type denote-date denote-subdirectory denote-rename-file denote-dired-rename-files
title: How to Use Denote

content: The denote-templates optionUsers can start a new note from a template. A template can be configured like this: elisp swift (setq denote-templates '((report . "* Some heading * Another heading") (memo . "* Some heading * Another heading))) A better approach is to use concat to join strings together: emacs-lisp swift (setq denote-templates `((report . "* Some heading * Another heading") (memo . ,(concat "* Some heading" " " "* Another heading" " "))))
title: How to Use Denote

content: Maintaining separate note-directory silosThe idea of silos means that notes are stored in different places, and those directories are separated from one another. We call these directories silos. To create a silo, first create a .dir-locals.el file inside the note directory. When creating it, Emacs will ask whether to store this path variable; choose yes. Write the following into .dir-locals.el: emacs-lisp wasm ;;; Directory Local Variables. For more information evaluate: ;;; ;;; (info "(emacs) Directory Variables") ((nil . ((denote-directory . "/path/to/silo/") (denote-infer-keywords . nil)))) Here is my notes directory (01Index is my denote-directory): bash lua ./01Index/ ├── .DS_Store ├── 20240228T160157--mastering-eshell__emacs_linux_shell.assets ├── 20240228T160157--mastering-eshell__emacs_linux_shell.org ├── 20240315T225352--c语言程序设计现代方法__c.org ├── 20240317T201104--cpp-primer__cpp.org ├── 20240407T230222--西方哲学史__reading.org └── ltximg ./02Area/ ├── .dir-locals.el ├── 20240201T021341--emacs-manual-note__emacs.assets ├── 20240201T021341--emacs-manual-note__emacs.org ├── 20240302T153745--计算机网络原理__network.assets ├── 20240302T153745--计算机网络原理__network.org ├── 20240320T175109--操作系统__operatingsystem.assets ├── 20240320T175109--操作系统__operatingsystem.org ├── 20240328T141613--计算机系统结构__computerarchitecture.assets └── 20240328T141613--计算机系统结构__computerarchitecture.org ./03Resource/ ├── .dir-locals.el ├── 20240221T162109--kde-plasma-桌面-korganizer-等软件关闭后仍然占用约-800-m-内存__bug_kde.assets ├── 20240221T162109--kde-plasma-桌面-korganizer-等软件关闭后仍然占用约-800-m-内存__bug_kde.org └── 20240222T000349--opensuse-tumbleweed-和-kde-的个人安装过程__kde_linux_opensuse.org ./04Project/ ├── .dir-locals.el └── 20240417T220159--denote-使用方法__emacs.org After that, we still need a custom user command that selects the silo automatically. In normal use, denote will put notes into 01Index by default. When we switch to 02Area, Denote-related commands will automatically store notes in the current silo. My user configuration is as follows: emacs-lisp perl (defvar my-denote-silo-directories `("/home/MEGA/02Area" "/home/MEGA/03Resource" "/home/MEGA/04Project" ;; You don't actually need to include the `denote-directory' here ;; if you use the regular commands in their global context. I am ;; including it for completeness. ,denote-directory) "List of file paths pointing to my Denote silos. This is a list of strings.") (defvar my-denote-commands-for-silos '(denote denote-date denote-subdirectory denote-template denote-type) "List of Denote commands to call after selecting a silo. This is a list of symbols that specify the note-creating interactive functions that Denote provides.") (defun my-denote-pick-silo-then-command (silo command) "Select SILO and run Denote COMMAND in it. SILO is a file path from `my-denote-silo-directories', while COMMAND is one among `my-denote-commands-for-silos'." (interactive (list (completing-read "Select a silo: " my-denote-silo-directories nil t) (intern (completing-read "Run command in silo: " my-denote-commands-for-silos nil t)))) (let ((denote-directory silo)) (call-interactively command))) The implementation of silos is not especially elegant. It is a relatively new feature, so for now we can only make do with it. I think the author should probably integrate this distributed-repository concept into the configuration around denote-directory. After all, I cannot always put all notes in one folder, and always using denote-subdirectory is not a great solution either.
title: How to Use Denote

content: Note File Naming ConventionNotes are stored in denote-directory, which defaults to ~/Documents/notes. By default, Denote follows this naming convention: text ini DATE==SIGNATURE--TITLE__KEYWORDS.EXTENSION The DATE field represents the date in year-month-day format, followed by an uppercase T for the current time. DATE also serves as the unique identifier of each note file. The SIGNATURE field contains an alphanumeric string. A signature has no predefined purpose; it is defined by the user. One possible use is to establish ordering relationships between files, such as 1, 1a, 1b, 1b1, 1b2, and so on. The TITLE field is lowercase by default and uses hyphens. The KEYWORDS field consists of one or more entries separated by underscores; when entering KEYWORDS, use commas as separators. Examples: text css 20220610T043241--initial-thoughts-on-the-zettelkasten-method__notetaking.org 20220610T062201--define-custom-org-hyperlink-type__denote_emacs_package.md 20220610T162327--on-hierarchy-and-taxis__notetaking_philosophy.txt denote-prompts can be configured in the following forms: text sql DATE.EXT DATE--TITLE.EXT DATE__KEYWORDS.EXT DATE==SIGNATURE.EXT DATE==SIGNATURE--TITLE.EXT DATE==SIGNATURE--TITLE__KEYWORDS.EXT DATE==SIGNATURE__KEYWORDS.EXT
title: How to Use Denote

content: Linking Notesdenote provides several commands for creating links between notes. All linked files must be Denote files; other files cannot be recognized.
title: How to Use Denote

content: Adding a single linkThe denote-link command selects a file in the minibuffer and inserts it into the current file as a link. The link format depends on whether the current file is Org or Markdown. In Org files, links to headings are also supported through the denote-org-store-link-to-heading command. Other file formats are not supported. When denote-link is called with a prefix argument (C-u by default), it sets the link to the [[denote:IDENTIFIER]] format regardless of file type. By default, the link description is determined as follows:
title: How to Use Denote

content: The denote-org-store-link-to-heading user optionThe option denote-org-store-link-to-heading determines whether org-store-link links to the current heading in an Org file. org-store stores the current link so it can later be inserted with org-insert-link, and the denote-org-store-link-to-heading option affects this command. When this option is non-nil, org-store-link stores the link to the current heading instead of the Denote file itself. If the heading does not have a CUSTOM_ID, Denote creates a PROPERTIES drawer and includes it in the heading. If a CUSTOM_ID already exists, org-store-link uses it as is.
title: How to Use Denote

content: Inserting a link to a heading inside an Org fileAs part of the denote-org-extras.el extension, denote-org-extras-link-to-heading provides links to headings inside Org files. The link takes this form: text lua [[denote:IDENTIFIER::#ORG-HEADING-CUSTOM-ID]][Description::Heading text]]
title: How to Use Denote

content: Inserting links that match a regular expressionThe command denote-add-links adds multiple links that match a regular expression. These links are inserted as a list, for example: text diff - link1 - link2 - link3 Examples of usable regular expressions: journal matches all notes whose title contains journal. _journal matches all notes whose keyword is journal. ^2022.*_journal matches all notes that start with 2022 and contain the keyword journal. \.txt matches files with the txt extension.
title: How to Use Denote

content: Linking to an existing note or creating a new oneIn a note-taking workflow, you may be developing one topic and want to connect it to another. Denote provides several convenient commands: denote-link-after-creating Create a new note in the background and link to it directly. denote-link-after-creating-with-command This command is similar to the previous one, but it prompts for the command used to create the note. denote-link-or-create Use denote-link on the target file, creating it if necessary.
title: How to Use Denote

content: The backlinks bufferdenote-backlinks generates a custom buffer that shows backlinks to the current note. A backlink is a link that points back to the current entry. By default, the backlinks buffer is designed to show the names of files that contain annotations linked to the current entry. Each file name is displayed on its own line. text lua Backlinks to "On being honest" (20220614T130812) ------------------------------------------------ 20220614T145606--let-this-glance-become-a-stare__journal.txt 20220616T182958--feeling-butterflies-in-your-stomach__journal.txtx The backlinks buffer uses Emacs’ built-in Xref infrastructure. On some operating systems, users may need to add certain executables to the relevant environment variables.
title: How to Use Denote
